Proposal Briefing · Microsoft Fabric

A centralised data platform for Mahitahi Trust

Bring our data together once — owned, governed, and reused for reporting, analytics and AI. One trusted source beneath the dashboards we already use.

Platform
Microsoft Fabric
Delivers
Warehouse + Lakehouse
Approach
Three phases
Status
For decision
What it is

One owned platform

A single, Mahitahi-owned data platform on Microsoft Fabric — a warehouse for reliable reporting and a lakehouse for advanced analytics — fed automatically from the systems we already run.

Why now

Data lives in silos

Our information is scattered across separate vendor systems. Reporting across them is manual and slow, and there is no single trusted version of the truth.

The shape of it

Delivered in phases

We start with priority systems (Precoro, Elmo) for early consolidated reporting, then extend to all systems, then enable AI and near real-time analytics.

01

Where we are today

Mahitahi runs on a number of specialised third-party systems. Each holds a different part of our organisational information — and each is managed by its own vendor, in its own structure.

Recordbase · service delivery & whaiora Precoro · Flexifund expenditure Elmo · workforce & HR + history LogiQC · policies & quality Argus · vehicle & GPS Jotform · osTicket · forms & support
!

Data is fragmented across systems that don't naturally connect.

!

Reporting across systems means manual extraction, cross-checking and reconciliation.

!

Cross-system analysis — like linking funding to service outcomes — is slow or not possible.

!

Historical, predictive and advanced analytics can't be done across our tools.

!

We have limited control over how our data is accessed, retained and moved between vendors.

!

Power BI presents data well — but only from one system at a time. There's no foundation beneath it.

Today Mahitahi has the reading room, but not the library behind it.

Power BI is the window we view data through — not the place it lives
02

The proposal

Establish a centralised data platform on Microsoft Fabric. Because Mahitahi already works within the Microsoft ecosystem, Fabric lets us build this in a familiar environment, with native links to our existing tools. It brings business intelligence and AI together in one governed place.

CapabilityFabric componentWhat it lets Mahitahi do
Centralised storageOneLakeOne open, organisation-owned store for all Mahitahi data — ending the current silos.
Automated pipelinesData FactoryPull and refresh data from Precoro, Elmo (incl. history), osTicket, Recordbase, Argus and Jotform into one place.
Data lakehouseData EngineeringHold raw and historical data of any type, ready for advanced analytics, ML and data science.
Data warehouseSynapse WarehouseStructured, modelled data optimised for fast, reliable business reporting.
Reporting & dashboardsPower BI · TableauTrusted dashboards for leadership, funders and operational teams — all from a single source.
Advanced analytics & MLData ScienceBuild predictive models — forecasting demand, flagging risks, surfacing service trends.
Real-time + batchReal-Time IntelligenceCombine scheduled data with live streams — e.g. Argus GPS or support tickets — for timely insight.
Automated KPI alertsData ActivatorAutomatically notify the right stakeholders when a service or contract KPI crosses its threshold — e.g. spend nearing budget, a deliverable slipping, or a service target missed.
Governance & securityPurview · Entra IDCentral access control, data lineage and compliance, using identities Mahitahi already manages.
03

How the platform works

Data flows through four stages — from our source systems, through automated pipelines, into a central store, and out to the people and tools that use it.

Stage 1

Source systems

Precoro, Elmo, Recordbase, Argus, Jotform, osTicket and the rest — where data is created day to day.

›
Stage 2

Ingest

Data Factory pipelines pull data on a schedule or in real time, cleaning and standardising it on the way in.

›
Stage 3

Store & model

OneLake holds it all: the lakehouse for raw & historical data, the warehouse for modelled reporting data.

›
Stage 4

Consume

Power BI & Tableau dashboards, advanced analytics, ML, data science and near real-time insight.

Both the warehouse and lakehouse sit on the same OneLake foundation — so data is stored once, and used many times.

04

A phased delivery

We build in stages so value lands early and risk stays manageable — each phase delivers something useful on its own.

Phase 1 · Foundation

Priority systems

Stand up the Fabric platform and build automated pipelines for priority systems — Precoro and Elmo — delivering early consolidated reporting.

Early consolidated reporting
Phase 2 · Integration

Full ingestion & history

Extend ingestion to the remaining systems and establish the lakehouse for deep historical and advanced analysis.

All systems · single source of truth
Phase 3 · Advanced

Real-time analytics & live dashboards

Live dashboards bringing together information from across Mahitahi's different platforms — and the capability for analysts to perform advanced analytics and run machine learning models.

Live dashboards · advanced analytics · ML
05

What Mahitahi gains

✓

Faster, more confident decisions at leadership and Board level, from consistent and timely information.

✓

More efficient funding and compliance reporting, with stronger evidence for funders.

✓

Automatic alerts to the right people when a KPI tied to their service or funding contract goes off track — no manual monitoring needed.

✓

Less kaimahi time spent compiling reports by hand — releasing capacity for higher-value mahi.

✓

New capability for historical, predictive and advanced analytics we can't deliver today.

✓

A clearer link between the resources we invest and the outcomes we achieve for whānau.

✓

Reduced risk and dependency on individual vendors for access to our own data.

✓

A scalable foundation for future machine learning and AI as the organisation matures.

06

Investment & licensing

Two ongoing costs run the platform: the Fabric capacity (the compute that powers everything) and Power BI licences for the people who use it. Storage is billed only for what we use. The choice of capacity size — and how we licence viewers — is where the budget is won.

⚑
Indicative figures only. Costs below are indicative NZD — converted from US list prices at roughly 1.72 (June 2026) and shown excluding GST — based on Mahitahi's actual team: 2 report builders and ~50 report users. Power BI Pro is ~NZ$24/user/month (current rate). Our New Zealand region, data volume and any Microsoft agreement will adjust the totals; confirmed numbers come with the detailed costing plan.
TodayPro only · no platform F8 + Pro RecommendedFull platform F64Free viewers · not needed yet
Fabric capacity / mo (reserved)—~NZ$1,070~NZ$8,580
Computenone8 CUs64 CUs
Power BI accesseach user needs Pro (~$24)each user needs Pro (~$24)viewers free · builders need Pro
Pro licences (2 builders + ~50 users)~NZ$1,250~NZ$1,250~NZ$50 (builders only)
OneLake storagen/a~NZ$40 / TB~NZ$40 / TB
Approx. total / month~NZ$1,250~NZ$2,320~NZ$8,630
Warehouse · lakehouse · pipelines✗✓✓
Copilot in Fabric✗✗✓ (F64 min)
Single governed source of truth✗✓✓
Right-sized for Mahitahi

Why a small capacity

With 2 builders and ~50 report users, ~52 Pro licences (~NZ$24 each) cost about NZ$1,250/month. A small F8 capacity adds the full platform — warehouse, lakehouse and pipelines — for ~NZ$1,070/month. That's roughly NZ$2,320/month all up: a fraction of a large capacity, with no loss of capability.

The F64 threshold

Why not F64 yet

Below F64, each report user needs a Pro licence — the same as today. F64 makes viewing free, but its unlimited-viewer benefit only pays for itself past about 180 report users. At ~50, we'd spend ~NZ$8,580/month on capacity to save ~NZ$1,200 in licences. We revisit F64 when users approach ~180, we want Copilot, or Phase 3 workloads grow.

Note: the "Today" column looks cheapest only because it buys no platform — it's the status quo this proposal exists to replace, not a real alternative. Even on F64, people who build reports still need a Pro licence; only viewers become free.

F8 vs F64 · capability at a glance

FeatureF8 RecommendedF64
Compute power8 CUs64 CUs
Relative size1×8× larger
Monthly cost (approx., reserved)~NZ$1,070~NZ$8,580
Data pipelinesGood for small–medium workloadsHandles large-scale workloads
Concurrent usersTens of usersHundreds to thousands
Data refreshesSuitable for daily refreshesFrequent / large refreshes
Lakehouse & warehouse performanceModerateMuch faster
Notebooks / Spark jobsSmall–medium workloadsLarge data-engineering workloads
Copilot in Fabric✗ No✓ Yes (min F64)
Power BI viewer licensingEveryone needs ProViewers free

For Mahitahi's Phase 1 — Precoro and Elmo, daily refreshes and ~50 report users — F8 sits comfortably in its range. F64's extra headroom only becomes necessary as workloads, refresh frequency or user numbers grow.

07

Recommendation

We recommend that Mahitahi Trust:

  1. Establish a centralised data platform on Microsoft Fabric, providing both a data warehouse and a data lakehouse.
  2. Use it to consolidate data from our operational systems and power reporting, advanced analytics, machine learning and real-time insight.
  3. Start on a small F8 capacity with Pro licences for the ~52 people who need access (2 builders + ~50 report users), validating real usage on the free 60-day trial before committing, then move to a 1-year reservation.
  4. Proceed to a phased delivery and costing plan, developed with the ITC Manager.

Next step

Confirm support to develop the detailed delivery plan and costings for Senior Leadership.
